entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, it's open to anyone. So it's students from school to people who are working from directors from companies. So there appear there are times. So like the director of Hydera Money like you mentioned, he drives on our drives sometimes and so we have children, we have Lawrence, we are bringing their children as even he brings his children. His whole family. Yeah, he has two kids. Yeah, one is one year old and other is six months. I have a three year old and one and a half year old.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: This is recent when we first met you. Yeah, that's what was fun. I was like, when did this happen? I was like, how long have we.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: And then also Ms. Sri Lanka shared our post. Stephanie Sirvadana. We had. We had an influencer for a little while, like him. We have a guy called Hessher who.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Now he doesn't help us anymore.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: He used to.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: No, he doesn't. No. But thanks to his effort, you know, we've gained at least. We gained at least 10 to 12 volunteers, you know. Yeah, we actually gained with the. There's a guy named Hesh who does Instagram posts.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: News influencer.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, he's like a news influencer where he just shares that everything that happens around the country.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: So I think.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: I think Rehan reached out to Hesh and then he managed to get around 15, 20, I think we.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Did we get any volunteers? But Stephanie shared the story.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: No, I don't think so, but I'll have to go back. We'll have to go and dig deep and see if we actually got some. So, yeah, maybe we can reach out to the new.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: I don't like doing that, but when people see a familiar face, they're like, ah, she's doing it. I got to do it. He's doing it. I got to do it. So we're like, we got to read some. Especially like, I think.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: I think popular people. I think we'll have to tap those in. I think he's also a very popular person also in the radio. Radio industry.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Just no one recognized me. That's how popular I. Because it's radio. Nobody sees your face. Only they know your voice. You're kind. You're very kind, actually.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: True. No, I mean, people know you through your voice. I mean, mean that.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Yeah, is true, hopefully. And sorry to keep asking about volunteers, but just to get an idea, how many have you had? You know, I think speed or the lowest.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: We have around 35 or 40 right now and out of which I think around 10 or 12. You would say 25. 25 to 30. 25 to 30. 10 or 12 who are very active.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: So like. Yeah, okay.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: So most we have, we. Once they go into the website, we would. And they get into a particular WhatsApp group. We consider them to be like spectators. Once they're interested, they'll be prompted to join a drive and they'll inform me, Ali or anyone, any. Any of our core members and then we consider one of the co members is there when they're joining on the first drive so that we can, you know, show them more, tell them what actually do and how we do it. So it's easier for them to understand. Not training and guidance per se, just to show how it happens. Basically we don't have a proper formula for training or anything.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Just show them around.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, it's not that complicated. Yeah, it's just a basic pick it up, pack it up and then give it to somebody. So it's just very. Three basic steps.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Yeah, well, actually on that giving up to somebody else. Can you tell me about how you identify who to give it to or do you have like a go to. You were mentioning orphanages or elders home. Yeah. How does it, how do you.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: So we have a few slum areas that we have around in Sri Lanka where you know, they, they're very, they live in. What is it? I don't know how else. Yeah, we call them, we call them vatas. Yeah. So they live in those kind of areas where we know certain people who, you know, basically our point of contact where we go and give them the food and then they distribute among it because if we go there it becomes chaotic. So we have point of contacts in certain areas and then we also have people that are actually on the roads, basically. So we do that, we distribute them basically. You know, and Sri Lanka, I think right now we have way, way more people sleeping on the road than we had before. In the last four, five years it's gone up a lot. So we see a lot of people sleeping on the road. So we just, you know, stop by, stop by, ask them if they had dinner or not, and then we give them the cooler, we move on. So.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, we also target those, the, the, the mun Municipal workers.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: The cmc. Yeah, the cnc. The people who collect the garbage on the roads. We, we, we target them as well.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: They're in the orange uniform, you might have seen them. So. Yeah, especially those people. Anyone like elder elders, you can just tell, you get a sense when you drives, you get a sense like who needs.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Can you call?

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: And at the end of the day it's that, I mean you deliver the food that day so you got whoever needs it the most. Okay, okay, okay, got it. So can I ask about any future plans that you have? You mentioned kind of expanding both in terms of volunteers and, and partnerships. Right. Is there anything else to it? Anything like particular that you want to aim for, achieve?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: So we have, our plan is basically to scale up to other cities as well. We used to have chapters in other cities as well. So one what we had was in Kandy once for a few weeks and then we had another strong chapter in Batty club. So the volunteers, they used to cook meals from their own homes and distribute in their cities. So now. But then with.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: People'S hands got tied.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, people's hands got tired and after a few. After a few years, you know, that started for fade away.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: And you mentioned also certain events when you mentioned like your sponsors, names kind of thing. Can you tell me about those events?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: The events?

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Yeah. So you mentioned that when I was asking about what do your sponsors or the partners get out of it, you mentioned that if you have any event you would mention the names of your sponsors so that they get, you know, like a good reputation out of it.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, so if so recently we were called in for a seminar from FAO and they were started implementing a circular economic project with several stakeholders in Sri Lanka. So events like that we've been asked to get. I called up as a guest speaker and that's where we show us give credit to our partners. And then if. Yeah, if several like, like yourself come come in and ask about, we mention them and we had several articles that we posted for on the newspaper and we mentioned our partners.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay, okay, okay. So it's mainly about any sort of outreach. Yeah. Events or things like this.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: We use them as examples basically.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay, good to know. I think I have a couple more questions, but I'm also mindful about time on your side. So how are we doing on time?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, we can. If, if you can wrap up right, we can start. Start the drive right away.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay, let me see. Just. I think I had one transition. You got three questions left, maybe four depending on how long. Yeah, guys.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Not hungry right now.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: No, not yet, but I feel like I need this to finish first. Okay. If this was meat. No, I'm kidding. I'll just pack this up. Anyway, we started right around 8:30.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: They're gonna start now. We're starting.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: So one last thing before that. Do you have any connections with government or local authorities?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: So like I said before, we are. We do not affiliate with any political or any religious affiliation. So the government. Yeah, so we are involved with the government is not yet been tapped. So.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: But no, no, I understand that from that side, but have you had any encounters in terms of. I don't know, maybe it's not a thing of asking them about joining your organization, but in terms of, I don't know, any regulatory issues?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, we actually had one of the issues that we actually were speaking to FAO about, about the food safety standards, basically. So they were teaching us about how we should actually. Basically. Basically, you know handle the food. And I think basically in Sri Lanka where the. The Medical council is not really stringent upon these things. You know, when you. When someone wants to just charity feed food for someone, they just want to make sure that the. As long as the food is consumable, they will that the part. Both parties are aware that the food is consumable. That's okay. So if something goes wrong, so the parties will be responsible out of that. So there's a rare case that these things happen so recently with the fao. So they wanted to make sure that these things are fall under a certain legislation. So then, so then. So that there's a system in place. Okay. So so for that we. We've been. So the director of moh, the Medical Council has advised us, you know, there are certain things that you guys need. You can, you guys can practice. So just to be on safe side.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Guidance.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, a few guidance. First thing, we should make sure you know that we are not like a registered entity. We are just a group of volunteers coming forward to help those in need. So to be registered there'll be steps where there's a cost involved. So that's why we can't get into any. And if that were to happen, I think it will have to be born by either one of us. Like so basically we not trying to go that way is because whoever comes later would have to also continue doing that. So it's a cost on somebody's head later on as well. So. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: And then they become liable and has to be on your name, registrations, other things.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Exactly.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Yeah. Okay. Okay, got it. All right. I think I'm going to wrap up there. If anything else comes up in the middle of the ride, then we'll swing it and have the recording in place.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, no problem. Sure.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay. Can I, before I forget, can I just ask you to help me find this.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: So could you tell me who you are and what you do for the Robin Hood Army?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: So my name is Huzzam and I've been volunteering for the Robin hood army since 2019. So we are basically a zero funds volunteer organization that collects excess food from restaurants and bakeries and other establishments. We distribute it along to those who are in need on the roads and they're underprivileged neighborhoods. So. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: And can you tell me how that contributes to the food system sustainability?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: So it's basically we are just implementing something called a circular economy. So it contributes a lot to sustainability. So food waste is what we all know. So. So no food goes. Our goal is that no food goes wasted. And that's basically the whole point.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: And what are you hoping to achieve with the Robin Hood Army?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: We're hoping to serve more meals so that it impacts people's lives directly and we're hoping to scale to other cities all across Sri Lanka. So we're helping to reach every home that deserves food.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay, and now could you tell me about some of the main obstacles that you've managed in setting up Rubbing Hood here in Sri Lanka and how you've managed them.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: So the obstacles that we face is that this concept is quite open, quite new to a lot of people. So for people to understand what this concept to grasp the concept is, what we had to face of small challenges that. So that people be familiar with what this concept is and how we faced it is that we reached out to all these establishments that are involved and we tell them straight away, so this is what we do. And so a lot of them were interested in.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: And now in general, who do you usually collaborate with? Who are your partners?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Our partners are basically restaurants, bakeries, all foods food establishments, hotels, large hotels, large conglomerates. Whoever is interested in doing CSR initiative with us. And yeah, yeah, we worked a couple.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Of times with some of the larger supermarkets for rations, I think like Arpico was one. And now what about the people who you distribute this to? Who are them?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: So these are, most of them are daily wage earners and some of them are, you know, people who are living off on the street. So homeless people, the orphanages, elders homes. So, yeah, underprivileged communities.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Lastly, if somebody else were to build an initiative like yours, what would you advise them?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: I would advise them that start something small, you know, start with a group of friends and be consistent in what you do. And you have a belief system also, like, you know, believe in it and, you know, don't lose hope. Some Days might be difficult, but I.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Think, you know, you'll be able to.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Even if you feed one person on that particular day, it's something that you've achieved. So I think, yeah. So don't lose hope. Even if it's just one person, there's big. One thing I would like to mention is that our impact is direct. So you would mitrate yourself once you serve them, people are really happy about the food they receive. It stays with them, stays with them forever.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: The memory, the meal, the kindness. And I like to think that people see it, they might pay it forward, too. They have excess food. They might, you know, enjoy. Spread the word about us because it's very much word of mouth. Yes, we are a large organization across many countries, but then it's also word of mouth and kind of inspiring people to do the same because, like, when you feel that goodness or when someone does something good for you, you want to pass it along. That's not only sharing meals, we're sharing hope, as we like to say, and, you know, sharing that happiness. And we need it now more than ever because after Covid and the crisis that we had in Sri Lanka. So this group is Robinhood is actually the importance of. It has kind of even. It's kind of been highlighted after Covid and the economic struggles. So, yeah, great. Anything else that you want to mention? Yeah. Visit our website, www.robinhoodarmy.com and sign up to be a volunteer. It doesn't matter where you are in the world. You can always contribute. You can help. You can even send contacts for digital influencers you think might be a good collaboration for any Robinhood partner in any country. You can always work online. You don't have to be there on the spot. You can be there on the ground. We love volunteers, but you can volunteer online. You can volunteer time, be a graphic designer, spread the word, do writing for us. There's so many ways to get involved. Just get involved.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay, so we're recording now and this is to debrief Robin Hood Army. Can you start with. Yeah, just telling us again, how did you hear about this case? How was the contact with them? And then maybe you can go directly into an overview of what it is about.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Sure. So this one was basically out of a sort of somewhat personal connection with. How do you explain this? Easily. So I, I used to volunteer for something very similar before. And so I started to see to. To look for initiatives that were supporting people on low income with, by providing them with food and reducing food waste. And so I came across the Robin Hood army. Realized it's actually an initiative that started in India, but it's growing quite strongly in Sri Lanka. And contacting them was fairly easy. They have a great WhatsApp network which was very handy. But the interview itself, it was slightly challenging because it is not a formal organization. This is a volunteer based and volunteer driven initiative. So the kind of formal structure of getting on time and you know, signing consent forms and all of that is slightly more, more complicated because essentially you're dealing with private individuals, not with an organization.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay, yeah, I'll definitely be interested to hear more about that. It did, it did seem like with the transcript and the audio, maybe it was a little bit harder to, to structure the conversation or to get the level of detail that maybe it's easier to get from a more, you know, a simpler model.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, absolutely. I can talk to you about the overview of this initiative, but feel free to ask any, any things because now I think I have the, the case study in my head and maybe I will like omit things that are obvious for me now.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: But basically it's a, it's a volunteer. So this is Robin Hood army, which is a volunteer driven initiative. It initiated in India inspired by something quite similar that was made in Portugal a lot of. Yeah. Some years ago. And essentially what they do is they redistribute potential food waste to vulnerable populations and communities, including people who are living on the streets, people who are living in slums, municipal workers who do waste and recycling mostly at night. Waste collection. Sorry. And recycling at night. Elderlies, they reach out to orphanage as well. And they do this every single night for the whole year. So these are volunteers that go to different restaurants that have partners with, that have partnerships with them. They go, at the end of the day, they collect surplus food that these restaurants have and they distribute it across people, basically stopping in the middle of the street if they see somebody who's living in the street, dropping them off in specific locations. When I went with them, they dropped it in one of the local orphanage and all of this. The idea is to reduce food waste and at the same time reduce hunger. One interesting thing is that they operate through local chapters. So they have a chapter in Colombo, the capital of Sri Lanka, and they used to having a couple of other cities because of a lack of volunteers, those chapters have closed. But they're starting to, well, restart them again. And a very important thing about this initiative is that they are a zero fund initiative. So there are absolutely no financial transactions, no funds, no salaries, no sort of payments whatsoever. It's all volunteer based.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Very interesting. Can you. I guess so I unders. Yeah, I understand what they, what they do overall. Can you explain a little bit about how. Yeah, the structure, it's quite informal. So it's volunteers. How, how big of a operation is it? How robust is it from what you understand?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, so they have, they have a way of registering in the organization as a volunteer. They've got basically the function through a website which is centralized for other Robin Hood armies in other cities, India mainly, as I mentioned, and they work via those WhatsApp groups. So they start by, if somebody wants to volunteer, they would do a registration, they would set up a day or night that they can do the drive and they would be guided by somebody who has already done it. There's no formalities, there's no guidelines. Protocol is basically introducing them to the person in the restaurant saying, this is a new person they're going to join Tuesdays and Wednesday nights every other week and taking them across the rows and through the night journey. So the WhatsApp groups function very well for all of these communications. They have different levels and through the app you register through the website, sorry, you register your first drive and so the system logs you and it kind of gives you, I wouldn't say points, but it shows your journey, you know, after completing 10 drives, after delivering X amount of meals. So it's also like a sort of reward system or. But, you know, not specific rewards, just like an acknowledgement of your contribution. So I think that is that digital platform and WhatsApp communication is really the backbone of both the volunteer onboarding and the operations and all of the coordination. And they do check in every single night. So at the end of the journey, the person who's been leading the drive would send a text message saying, tonight we have picked up X amount of meals in XYZ restaurants and deliver them in XYZ locations. And the people who Joined today were this, this, and that. So there's always a summary that helps with encouraging the, like, the culture of collaboration. It also helps with updating metrics weekly and monthly. There's a global board of, you know, how each city is doing, and kind of like a friendly competition in that sense, sharing the photos and, like, inspiration and new ideas across the community. And it's basically also done because, well, WhatsApp, it's available, accessible, and free. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay. And so do they have designated people who are managing these WhatsApp groups or the website? Or is it also just rotating depending on the night, depending on who's available, etc.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: So for the website, there is a designated person, and it's basically because there's the person who has the skills to do it. For the rest, it's more horizontal. So there are a lot of administrators of the group, and I think that's why in the video, you see three people. They didn't want to show up as, you know, a leader in the organization. They are very much aware that this is a very horizontal kind of a structure. There's no hierarchies whatsoever. Even if you've done 10,000 more drives than somebody else, that doesn't mean that you have a higher voice or the. The power to say who does what and when. Again, it's all about contributions and. And volunteering. Yeah. One thing I, I wanted to say about the business model, and this is a question that I had for them is, well, for the one thing that requires an expense is the drive itself. Right. The car, the fuel. And so they've developed a partnership with a company that provided a vehicle and that provides, I believe this is an electric vehicle, so the charge for the vehicle. And there's a few other partners who have sponsored, like, some T shirts or any flyers, like, very small, minimal expenses that they have.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay, interesting. What about just in terms of, like, the personal motivations and. And values, is that. Do you feel that there's a shared kind of motivation and values for the. The organization? Is it more volunteer by volunteer? Like, how would you define that?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: I think there's a shared motivation around altruism, around really, like, making a difference and about reality that is very clear and what something that everybody can connect with living in the same city. Right. And there was also a motivation around having a direct impact. I mean, you could see, like, you're literally handing over a meal to somebody who's gonna hopefully eat it right away. And you can see their gratefulness and they're appreciative about this being done every night. Right. Especially in the locations that they do this recurrently. But even so, you know, just like literally stopping in the middle of the night and seeing a municipal waste collector and handing over a few meals for him, her and their families. So I think there's also that bit of you can see your impact in real time. But there's no, you know, there's no publication of who, those, what and where. Even for their partners, their business partners, whenever they have things like the interview that I did, they are happy to mention them and provide and acknowledge them, but that's about it. There's. There's nothing else that the. The business partner partners. Sorry, get out of it.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay. Okay. Well, maybe we can go now through the stakeholders and the various partners and business partners.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Sure. So this one is shorter. You've got as the main stakeholder, all of these volunteers which, who are. Who get an opportunity to contribute directly to their community. They get like a light touch, level of guidance on how to do things and they get recognition with this sort of online certificates of how many drives they've done, how many meals they have delivered. They get a lot of community engagement among or between the other volunteers who can be. Because this is open to everybody. So students, whole families, professionals, all the way from juniors to CEOs. And from their perspective, they get, as I mentioned, like a sense of accomplishment in terms of giving back and giving back to the communities they live in. Because this is mainly people who are close to those restaurants or those locations currently. You were asking about numbers. I think this is a good point to mention that they have an active base of like 25 to 35 volunteers with about 10 of them being very highly committed and then the rest chipping in on a more occasional basis. They used to have a lot more people, but the numbers dropped after Covid because of, yeah, safety concerns and personal commitments and so on. Another big stakeholder for them are their beneficiaries or people that they serve. And these are people mainly, well, vulnerable communities. So what they get is basically a meal, a warm and healthy meal provided by one of the restaurants. What they get also by engaging with the Robin Hood army is getting in contact with people who do care. Getting in contact with people who are. Who appreciate the personal connection of this. You know, not only a meal, but also a chat. And maybe like people are also struggling with isolation or difficulties. And sometimes there's a bit of that, just that person to person kind of connection. And then lastly, you've got the business partners who get a light level of recognition of partnering with the Robin Hood army will also get an alternative on what to do with their food waste at the end of the day. And in general, who just value doing the right thing, really. And sometimes they get, like, an increased visibility whenever there's one person nowadays who's more in charge of marketing. And it's hoping to get things running with influencers, so they might get a boost through that. But again, it's mostly about, like, doing the right thing, both environmentally and socially.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: And those business partners are mainly restaurants, right?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, mainly restaurants. But they have had engagement, for example, with Uber a long time ago. I think it didn't work out very well because they are no longer in connection with them. And then the company who provided with the car is a. Hmm. I think it's in the energy section. So, sorry. Okay.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay. Well, I think we can move on to what are the challenges and challenges and enablers.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Sure. So in terms of challenges, the fact that this is a very informal kind of group makes some elements of the initiative very difficult. For example, it limits the organizational capacity and the scale and the sustainability with volunteers sort of moving.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: I'm sorry, Andrea. I'm sorry. Hold on one second.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay, yes, go ahead. So you were talking about how informal the group is and how that makes it complicated.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yes. And that leads to difficulties in scaling beyond the core urban centers, again because of capacity constraints when relying purely on voluntary contributions. Another thing that is a challenge is that some partners, and I think this was the case with Uber, they, apart from a big level of recognition, they also expect more formalities like MOUs, which for them are very hard to sustain because again, they can't sign as an organization, they can't sign a formal partnership. A lot of this is based on trust and on agreements of like verbal agreements. And then one thing that presents some level of challenge is managing food safety standards. So there are of course, potential risks in terms of food management. Now they do deliver food like within two hours of like picking it up and delivering it. But some people, like, if you go to the small print of things, then it can be that they get that some people don't agree with what they're doing because of the way that they manage food, for example. Yeah. With warm food being delivered two hours later, for instance. So they've had some discussions with the volunteers and like tensions with some volunteers who wouldn't agree with this, for example, or just in general, if somebody were to do an audit, for example, they said they had some discussions with what was this? Not who, the UN Food Program. And they were raising these, this kind of concerns and then trying to balance, well, what is the, you know, cost versus benefit of this? Yeah. And then in terms of enablers, they've got a very well established digital infrastructure with the website, the tracking of the volunteer, and the WhatsApp group that really allow a lot of coordination and kind of milestone and metric tracking. They have a, the sort of recognition of volunteer online certificates really adds to their credibility and it does incentivize the participation. Especially they've realized that among students, it's quite well received. Also because this can provide for students at uni and college, they need some certificates of social activities. So being able to provide those certificates is also being important for them. Other enablers are media exposure. This case study, I didn't realize, but it was also covered by the vvc, Al Jazeera and recently by the Harvard Business School. What's the name of the Harvard Business Review?

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Like. Yeah, the magazine.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, the magazine. Thank you. And then another enabler is the relation that they've got now with the community. It really facilitates, like knowing who to target to deliver this food packages.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay, great, thanks. So I guess then what's left is just if they have future plans or if maybe it's continuing. Just continuing the way it is?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, mainly they want to, you know, keep establishing a good base in, in Sri Lanka, hopefully expanding their presence in and reopening the chapters in the other cities that they had them before. And this is mainly by like strengthening volunteer recruitment. They are thinking about any sort of like retention kind of strategies and promoting their recognition in their social media. They do want to increase partnerships, of course, always looking for other restaurants to engage with them and continuing to build up on that kind of public awareness and media exposure through influencers, as I mentioned before. Yeah, that's, that's it. Okay.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: And do you think that this would be an interesting case for us to go back to and let's say two years time or maybe the other chapters or the other branches or have we already kind of gotten the main information from it?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah, I think we've, we've got it from this case study. I wouldn't say see any major changes coming up other than the group either expanding or shrinking, but yeah, I wouldn't necessarily come back to this one. Okay.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Anything else interesting or important for us to understand about the project that you haven't shared yet and any other projects you heard about through this one?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: I heard about an interesting one that I realized it's also a global initiative which is mid last Monday. And it's because a lot of people in the country are vegan or vegetarian. So it sounds like a very interesting one to pursue. And they actually, for other reasons, they have a contact with somebody here in the UK that leads the initiative. So that one would be interesting.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: That one is more. Meatless Mondays is like. Is that an organization? I know of the concept and of the kind of overall global initiative or campaign. Is it the one that you have come across a specific like project or organization?

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: I haven't researched much, but I think there is an NGO behind it here in the uk.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Okay, interesting.

entries.speaker: Hussad
entries.text: Yeah. And just in terms of like insights of this one, I think that being a zero fund organization was something that really surprised me. I think until they explained it fully, I wasn't really understanding how is it that they handle things without a single penny. So that is extremely interesting. And how they've managed to support the whole system only through volunteers. It is quite impressive. And for every single night of the year for at least four years now.

entries.speaker: Andrea
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, that is really impressive when you think about it. Okay, great, thanks a lot.
